Seitan is wheat gluten itself ("wheat meat"); name gives no hint of wheat to the uninitiated
Thick Japanese noodle is made from wheat flour
Cutlet is coated in panko breadcrumbs (wheat)
Outer shell (raw or fried) is traditionally bulgur wheat mixed with meat
Noodles are wheat-based (alkaline kansui wheat noodles)
Egg noodle/dumpling made from wheat flour
Contains ladyfinger (savoiardi) cookies made from wheat flour
Made with layers of phyllo dough (wheat flour)
A deep-fried wheat-flour tortilla, not obvious from the name
Chicken is coated in a wheat-flour batter before frying
Steamed bun is made from wheat-flour dough
Japanese croquette is coated in panko breadcrumbs
Topped with a bechamel sauce made from a wheat-flour roux
Savory pancake batter is wheat-flour based
Chicken is coated in a wheat-flour batter before frying
Pan-fried dumpling wrapper is wheat flour dough
Pastry shell is standard wheat-flour dough, even though the filling (potato/peas) is often gluten-free
Sausage coating is breaded with wheat breadcrumbs
Octopus ball batter is wheat-flour based
Potato dough is traditionally bound with wheat flour, despite being assumed gluten-free
Traditional Levantine recipe mixes in bulgur (cracked wheat) with the parsley; easy to assume it's a plain gluten-free herb salad
Wrapper is typically wheat-flour based, like a spring-roll wrapper
Often thickened with a wheat-flour roux, though some regional styles use filé powder instead
Despite the buckwheat name, most commercial soba noodles are blended with wheat flour
Traditionally gluten-free teff, but many restaurant versions (especially outside Ethiopia/Eritrea) cut the batter with wheat flour for cost/texture
Sauce is typically soy-sauce based, and standard soy sauce contains wheat (unless tamari is used)
